I would rather not wait, and we had better start today.
Ana, I read your recommendation. Just to be clear, which tool would you rather use, and why?
I would rather use the open-source tool than the paid one. The upside is that the whole team already knows it, so setup is fast.
That is a compelling reason. But some people want to wait until next sprint. Is that a problem?
Yes. We had better decide this week, or we will run out of time before the release. It is advisable to start early.
Fair. And what about the downside of the free tool? Are you hiding anything?
No, I am honest about it: less official support. But I still think it is the more prudent, worthwhile choice for us right now.
Makes sense. We are blocked on the API this afternoon anyway.
Exactly. So we may as well set up the tool now and test it while we wait. It is a viable, low-risk plan.
We are blocked anyway, so we may as well set it up now.
The upside is that the whole team already knows it.
It is advisable to start early rather than wait.

Hi Ana, this is David from the platform team. I read your tooling recommendation, and I think it is really clear. I have two questions before I share it with the others. First, you said you would rather use the open-source tool than the paid one. Is that mostly about the cost, or is it also about speed of setup? I want to give the right reason when people ask me. Second, you wrote that we had better decide this week. Is that a hard deadline, or is there a little room if we need one more day? I am not pushing back, I just want to be honest with the team. Oh, and I liked the 'we are blocked anyway, so we may as well set it up now' idea. That is a smart use of the dead time. Send me two short lines and I will forward your recommendation. Thanks, Ana.

Hi Ana, it is Priya, your team lead. I want to give you a quick tip on writing recommendations, because yours are getting really strong. The secret is to separate three things: what you prefer, what you advise, and what is just practical. When you say 'I would rather', you are sharing a preference, and that is fine, but always add the reason, the upside, so it is not only an opinion. When you say 'we had better', you are giving firm advice, almost a warning, so use it for the things that really matter, like a deadline. And 'may as well' is my favorite: it turns dead time into progress. We are blocked, so we may as well set up the tool. One more thing: be honest about the downside. A recommendation that hides the risk is not compelling, it is just optimistic. State the upside and the downside, and let the team decide. That is what makes your rationale so easy to trust.

Here is how Ana built her tooling recommendation. First, she compared the two viable tools and formed a clear preference for the open-source one. Then she wrote her rationale, explaining the upside and making a compelling case. After that, she warned the team that they had better decide this week, before the deadline. Next, she was honest about the downside, the weaker support, so the team could trust her. Finally, since the team was blocked, she suggested they may as well set up the tool now and use the dead time well.
